channel.title: French Towns Lakehouse
channel.description: A data lakehouse for French municipal and administrative data

item.title: How Many Days Would it Take to Visit Every Town in France?
item.description:
Code 
"""
French Towns Visit-Time Calculator
───────────────────────────────────
Estimates how long it would take to visit every French commune, accounting
for population-driven dwell time, monument density, terrain difficulty,
island/ferry access, and intra/inter-department travel.
"""

import os
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import datetime

import duckdb
from IPython.display import display, HTML, Markdown

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import polars as pl
from dotenv import find_dotenv, load_dotenv
from great_tables import GT, loc, style

# ── Constants ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

MONUMENT_PENALTY_FLOOR = 5       # first N monuments get no penalty
MONUMENT_PENALTY_BONUS = 3       # flat bonus if > floor
MONUMENT_PENALTY_PER_EXTRA = 1   # per monument after floor
MONUMENT_PENALTY_CAP = 60

MOUNTAIN_MULT = 1.3
COAST_MULT = 1.15
ISLAND_COMMUNE_MULT = 1.5
MAX_MIN_PER_COMMUNE = 480  # 1 day cap

DRIVE_MIN_RURAL = 15
DRIVE_MIN_DENSE = 8
INTER_DEPT_DRIVE_H = 1.5
MAX_H_PER_DAY = 10

DENSE_DEPTS = {"75", "92", "93", "94", "69", "13", "59"}

# Overseas travel days per department: all groups 4 days, Corsica 1 day ferry
OVERSEAS_TRIPS: list[tuple[str, int, str]] = [
    ("971", 4, "Antilles"),
    ("972", 4, "Antilles"),
    ("977", 4, "Antilles"),
    ("978", 4, "Antilles"),
    ("973", 4, "Guyane"),
    ("974", 4, "Océan Indien"),
    ("976", 4, "Océan Indien"),
    ("975", 4, "Saint-Pierre-et-Miquelon"),
    ("987", 4, "Polynésie française"),
    ("988", 4, "Nouvelle-Calédonie"),
    ("986", 4, "Wallis-et-Futuna"),
    ("2A", 1, "Corse"),
    ("2B", 1, "Corse"),
]

# Single-commune islands (Îles du Ponant) → 0.5 extra day each
SINGLE_ISLAND_COMMUNES: set[str] = {
    "17004", "56088", "29082", "22016", "56069", "56085",
    "56086", "56087", "29084", "29155", "29083", "85113",
}

# Belle-Île → 0.5 extra day split across 4 communes
BELLE_ILE_COMMUNES: set[str] = {"56009", "56114", "56152", "56241"}

GOLD_TABLES = ["dim_communes", "dim_geography", "fact_population", "dim_commune_monument_stats"]

COMMUNE_QUERY = """
    SELECT
        c.id                              AS commune_id,
        c.name                            AS name,
        c.department_code                 AS department_code,
        c.department_name                 AS department_name,
        c.region_code                     AS region_code,
        c.region_name                     AS region_name,
        c.flag_metropole                  AS flag_metropole,
        g.is_mountain                     AS is_mountain,
        g.is_coast                        AS is_coast,
        g.is_insular                      AS is_insular,
        g.is_island_commune               AS is_island_commune,
        COALESCE(s.total_monuments, 0)    AS total_monuments,
        p.population                      AS population,
        ST_X(ST_GeomFromWKB(g.centroid_wkb)) AS longitude,
        ST_Y(ST_GeomFromWKB(g.centroid_wkb)) AS latitude
    FROM gold.dim_communes c
    LEFT JOIN gold.dim_geography g ON c.id = g.commune_id AND g.is_current
    LEFT JOIN gold.dim_commune_monument_stats s ON c.id = s.commune_id
    LEFT JOIN (SELECT id, population FROM gold.fact_population WHERE year = 2023) p
        ON c.id = p.id
    WHERE c.is_current
    ORDER BY c.department_code, c.name
"""


@dataclass
class TripTotals:
    """Final day-count breakdown for the whole tour."""
    mainland_days: float
    overseas_metro_days: float
    overseas_trip_days: int
    grand_total_days: float


# ── Environment & lakehouse connection ───────────────────────────────

def load_environment() -> None:
    """Load POLARIS_CLIENT_ID / POLARIS_CLIENT_SECRET from .env."""
    load_dotenv(find_dotenv())


def connect_to_lakehouse() -> duckdb.DuckDBPyConnection:
    """Open a DuckDB connection wired up to the Polaris/Iceberg catalog."""
    conn = duckdb.connect()
    conn.execute("LOAD iceberg;")
    conn.execute("LOAD spatial;")
    _create_polaris_secret(conn)
    _attach_polaris_catalog(conn)
    _create_gold_views(conn)
    return conn


def _create_polaris_secret(conn: duckdb.DuckDBPyConnection) -> None:
    conn.execute(f"""
        CREATE SECRET polaris_secret (
            TYPE iceberg,
            CLIENT_ID '{os.environ['POLARIS_CLIENT_ID']}',
            CLIENT_SECRET '{os.environ['POLARIS_CLIENT_SECRET']}',
            ENDPOINT 'http://localhost:8181/api/catalog'
        )
    """)


def _attach_polaris_catalog(conn: duckdb.DuckDBPyConnection) -> None:
    conn.execute("""
        ATTACH 'french_towns' AS polaris (
            TYPE iceberg,
            ENDPOINT 'http://localhost:8181/api/catalog',
            SECRET 'polaris_secret'
        )
    """)


def _create_gold_views(conn: duckdb.DuckDBPyConnection) -> None:
    """Expose each lakehouse table as a `gold.<table>` view."""
    conn.execute("CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS gold;")
    for table in GOLD_TABLES:
        conn.execute(f"CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW gold.{table} AS SELECT * FROM polaris.lakehouse.{table}")


def fetch_commune_data(conn: duckdb.DuckDBPyConnection) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Pull one row per commune with geography, population, and monument stats."""
    return conn.execute(COMMUNE_QUERY).pl()


# ── Per-commune visit-time pipeline ──────────────────────────────────

def compute_base_visit_minutes(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Assign a baseline visit duration (minutes) from the population tier."""
    return df.with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("population").is_null()).then(10)
        .when(pl.col("population") < 500).then(10)
        .when(pl.col("population") < 2_000).then(20)
        .when(pl.col("population") < 10_000).then(45)
        .when(pl.col("population") < 50_000).then(120)
        .when(pl.col("population") < 100_000).then(240)
        .otherwise(480)
        .alias("_base_minutes")
    )


def compute_monument_penalty(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Flat bonus + extra minute per monument beyond the floor, capped."""
    return df.with_columns(
        pl.min_horizontal(
            pl.when(pl.col("total_monuments") > MONUMENT_PENALTY_FLOOR)
            .then(MONUMENT_PENALTY_BONUS + (pl.col("total_monuments") - MONUMENT_PENALTY_FLOOR) * MONUMENT_PENALTY_PER_EXTRA)
            .otherwise(0),
            MONUMENT_PENALTY_CAP,
        ).alias("_monument_penalty")
    )


def _terrain_multiplier_for_row(row: dict) -> float:
    """Combine one commune's terrain-difficulty factors into a single multiplier."""
    multiplier = 1.0
    if row["is_mountain"]:
        multiplier *= MOUNTAIN_MULT
    if row["is_coast"]:
        multiplier *= COAST_MULT
    if row["is_island_commune"]:
        multiplier *= ISLAND_COMMUNE_MULT
    return multiplier


def compute_terrain_multiplier(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Attach the combined terrain-difficulty multiplier for each commune."""
    return df.with_columns(
        pl.struct(["is_mountain", "is_coast", "is_island_commune"])
        .map_elements(_terrain_multiplier_for_row, return_dtype=pl.Float64)
        .alias("_multiplier")
    )


def compute_island_extra_minutes(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Add fixed extra time for ferry-only single-commune islands and Belle-Île."""
    return df.with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("commune_id").is_in(SINGLE_ISLAND_COMMUNES)).then(300)
        .when(pl.col("commune_id").is_in(BELLE_ILE_COMMUNES)).then(75)
        .otherwise(0)
        .alias("_island_extra_min")
    )


def finalize_visit_minutes(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Combine base time, monument penalty, terrain multiplier and island extras
    into `visit_minutes` / `visit_hours`, then drop the intermediate columns."""
    df = df.with_columns(
        pl.min_horizontal(
            ((pl.col("_base_minutes") + pl.col("_monument_penalty"))
             * pl.col("_multiplier") + pl.col("_island_extra_min"))
            .cast(pl.Int64),
            MAX_MIN_PER_COMMUNE,
        ).alias("visit_minutes")
    )
    df = df.with_columns(
        (pl.col("visit_minutes") / 60).round(2).alias("visit_hours")
    )
    return df.drop(["_base_minutes", "_monument_penalty", "_multiplier", "_island_extra_min"])


def compute_visit_minutes(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Run the full per-commune visit-time pipeline end to end."""
    df = compute_base_visit_minutes(df)
    df = compute_monument_penalty(df)
    df = compute_terrain_multiplier(df)
    df = compute_island_extra_minutes(df)
    return finalize_visit_minutes(df)


# ── Overseas travel & filtering ──────────────────────────────────────

def build_overseas_lookup() -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Build a department → (travel days, trip group) lookup table."""
    return pl.DataFrame(
        [{"dept": dept, "days": days, "group": group} for dept, days, group in OVERSEAS_TRIPS],
        schema={"dept": pl.Utf8, "days": pl.Int64, "group": pl.Utf8},
    )


def attach_overseas_travel_days(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Join overseas trip metadata onto each commune by department code."""
    overseas_lookup = build_overseas_lookup()
    return (
        df.join(overseas_lookup, left_on="department_code", right_on="dept", how="left")
        .with_columns([
            pl.col("days").fill_null(0).alias("overseas_travel_days"),
            pl.col("group").fill_null("").alias("overseas_group"),
        ])
        .drop(["days", "group"])
    )


# ── Department-level aggregation ─────────────────────────────────────

def aggregate_by_department(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Roll up commune-level visit hours into one row per department."""
    return (
        df.group_by("department_code")
        .agg([
            pl.first("department_name").alias("department_name"),
            pl.first("region_name").alias("region_name"),
            pl.first("flag_metropole").alias("flag_metropole"),
            pl.len().alias("n_towns"),
            pl.sum("visit_hours").alias("total_visit_hours"),
            pl.max("overseas_travel_days").alias("overseas_travel_days"),
        ])
        .sort("department_code")
    )


def compute_intra_department_travel_hours(dept_df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Estimate driving time between towns within the same department."""
    return (
        dept_df.with_columns(
            pl.when(pl.col("department_code").is_in(DENSE_DEPTS))
            .then(DRIVE_MIN_DENSE)
            .otherwise(DRIVE_MIN_RURAL)
            .alias("_drive_min")
        )
        .with_columns(
            ((pl.col("n_towns") - 1) * pl.col("_drive_min") / 60)
            .round(1)
            .alias("intra_travel_hours")
        )
        .drop("_drive_min")
    )


def compute_department_totals(dept_df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Sum visit + intra-department travel time into total hours/days per department."""
    return dept_df.with_columns([
        (pl.col("total_visit_hours") + pl.col("intra_travel_hours"))
        .round(1).alias("total_hours"),
        ((pl.col("total_visit_hours") + pl.col("intra_travel_hours")) / MAX_H_PER_DAY)
        .round(1).alias("total_days"),
    ])


def build_department_summary(df: pl.DataFrame) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """Full department-level aggregation pipeline: group, then add travel/totals."""
    dept_df = aggregate_by_department(df)
    dept_df = compute_intra_department_travel_hours(dept_df)
    return compute_department_totals(dept_df)


# ── Trip-wide totals ──────────────────────────────────────────────────

def split_mainland_and_overseas(dept_df: pl.DataFrame) -> tuple[pl.DataFrame, pl.DataFrame]:
    """Split the department summary into metropolitan vs. overseas rows."""
    mainland = dept_df.filter(pl.col("flag_metropole") == 1)
    overseas = dept_df.filter(pl.col("flag_metropole") == 0)
    return mainland, overseas


def compute_overseas_trip_days() -> int:
    """Count each overseas trip group once (e.g. the 4 Antilles depts share one trip)."""
    unique_groups = {(group, days) for _, days, group in OVERSEAS_TRIPS}
    return sum(days for _, days in unique_groups)


def compute_inter_department_travel_hours(dept_df: pl.DataFrame) -> float:
    """Estimate driving time between departments within the same region."""
    return sum(
        max(0, len(group) - 1) * INTER_DEPT_DRIVE_H
        for _, group in dept_df.group_by("region_name")
    )


def compute_grand_totals(
    dept_df: pl.DataFrame,
    mainland: pl.DataFrame,
    overseas: pl.DataFrame,
) -> TripTotals:
    """Combine mainland touring, overseas-department touring, and overseas flight days."""
    inter_dept_h = compute_inter_department_travel_hours(dept_df)
    overseas_trip_days = compute_overseas_trip_days()

    mainland_total_h = mainland["total_hours"].sum() + inter_dept_h
    mainland_days = mainland_total_h / MAX_H_PER_DAY

    overseas_total_h = overseas["total_hours"].sum()
    overseas_metro_days = overseas_total_h / MAX_H_PER_DAY

    grand_total_days = mainland_days + overseas_metro_days + overseas_trip_days

    return TripTotals(
        mainland_days=mainland_days,
        overseas_metro_days=overseas_metro_days,
        overseas_trip_days=overseas_trip_days,
        grand_total_days=grand_total_days,
    )


# ── Orchestration ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

load_environment()
conn = connect_to_lakehouse()

df = fetch_commune_data(conn)
df = compute_visit_minutes(df)
df = attach_overseas_travel_days(df)

dept_df = build_department_summary(df)
mainland, overseas = split_mainland_and_overseas(dept_df)

totals = compute_grand_totals(dept_df, mainland, overseas)
mainland_total_days = totals.mainland_days
overseas_total_days = totals.overseas_metro_days
overseas_travel_total_days = totals.overseas_trip_days
grand_total_days = totals.grand_total_days

Have you ever wondered what it would take to visit every single town in France, all 35,000 of them?

Not just the big cities (Paris, Marseille, Lyon). Not just the Plus Beaux Villages de France. All of them. The tiny hamlets in the Massif Central, the remote Corsican mountain villages, and all the overseas territoise, such as the atolls of French Polynesia.

This is a rough, fun estimation. Let’s see how many days it would take.

Methodology

Per-town visit time is based on population, weighted by monuments, mountains, coast, and island logistics:

Population tier Base visit time + Monument penalty
Unknown / < 500 hab. 10 min +3 min if >5 monuments, +1 per extra (cap 60 min)
500 – 2 000 hab. 20 min same
2 000 – 10 000 hab. 45 min same
10 000 – 50 000 hab. 2 h same
50 000 – 100 000 hab. 4 h same
> 100 000 hab. 8 h same

Monuments shown are those listed in the French national registry of historical monuments (base Mérimée). Many communes have additional sites of interest not counted here, like churches, châteaux, archaeological sites. So the real “things worth seeing” is higher. The reason we include monumenrt count is because, why would you go to a french town and not visit its heritage (at least some of it)?

Multipliers: mountain towns × 1.3, coastal towns × 1.15, island communes × 1.5. This is because these territoris are usually less accessible and require more time for the visits.

Island logistics: single-commune islands (Île d’Yeu, Ouessant, etc.) get +0.5 days each; Belle-Île-en-Mer gets +0.5 days across its 4 communes. Larger islands with bridge/causeway connections (Ré, Oléron, Noirmoutier) have no extra penalty.

Travel time: 8–15 min drive between nearby towns within a department (denser = shorter), plus 1.5 h between department centres.

Overseas: each territory group gets 4 fixed travel days for flights + logistics. Corsica gets 1 ferry day. Per-town visit time is still fully counted.

No commune exceeds 1 day (480 min) — even the biggest cities.

All data comes from the lakehouse: population (2023), monument counts, mountain/coast/island flags, commune centroids, and administrative boundaries.

Top 10 Most Monument-Rich Towns

These are the communes with the most historical monuments — a major driver of visit time:

Code 
top10 = (df
    .sort("total_monuments", descending=True)
    .head(10)
    .select(["name", "department_name", "population", "total_monuments", "visit_hours"])
)

top10_display = top10.with_columns([
    pl.col("population").fill_null(0).cast(pl.Int64).alias("population"),
    pl.col("visit_hours").round(1).alias("visit_hours"),
])
top10_display.columns = ["Commune", "Département", "Population", "Monuments", "Heures"]

(
    GT(top10_display)
    .tab_header("Top 10 — most monuments per commune")
    .fmt_number(columns=["Population"], decimals=0)
    .fmt_number(columns=["Heures"], decimals=1)
    .tab_style(locations=loc.body(), style=style.text(weight="bold"))
    .cols_align("left", columns=["Commune", "Département"])
    .cols_align("center", columns=["Population", "Monuments", "Heures"])
)
Top 10 — most monuments per commune
Commune Département Population Monuments Heures
Paris Paris 2,103,778 1885 8.0
Bordeaux Gironde 267,991 384 8.0
Rochelle Charente-Maritime 79,851 294 5.8
Nancy Meurthe-et-Moselle 103,671 259 8.0
Lyon Rhône 519,127 259 8.0
Rouen Seine-Maritime 117,662 234 8.0
Toulouse Haute-Garonne 514,819 232 8.0
Strasbourg Bas-Rhin 293,771 232 8.0
Arras Pas-de-Calais 42,875 227 3.0
Dijon Côte-d'Or 161,830 216 8.0

By Region

How the total days break down by region:

Code 
region_agg = (df
    .group_by("region_name")
    .agg([
        pl.len().alias("n_towns"),
        pl.sum("visit_hours").alias("total_visit_h"),
    ])
)

region_rows = []
for r in region_agg.iter_rows(named=True):
    region = r["region_name"]
    depts_in_region = dept_df.filter(pl.col("region_name") == region)
    region_visit_h = r["total_visit_h"]
    region_travel_h = depts_in_region["intra_travel_hours"].sum()
    inter = max(0, len(depts_in_region) - 1) * 1.5
    total_h = region_visit_h + region_travel_h + inter
    region_rows.append({
        "region": region,
        "towns": r["n_towns"],
        "visit_h": round(region_visit_h, 0),
        "travel_h": round(region_travel_h + inter, 0),
        "total_days": round(total_h / 10, 1),
    })

region_df = pl.DataFrame(region_rows).sort("total_days", descending=True)
reg_tbl = region_df.rename({
    "region": "Région", "towns": "Communes",
    "visit_h": "Visite (h)", "travel_h": "Trajet (h)",
    "total_days": "Jours",
})

(
    GT(reg_tbl)
    .tab_header("Days required by region")
    .fmt_number(columns=["Communes", "Visite (h)", "Trajet (h)"], decimals=0)
    .fmt_number(columns=["Jours"], decimals=1)
    .cols_align("center")
)
Days required by region
Région Communes Visite (h) Trajet (h) Jours
Grand Est 5,115 1,533 1,290 282.2
Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 4,025 1,786 989 277.5
Occitanie 4,446 1,498 1,126 262.4
Nouvelle-Aquitaine 4,293 1,491 1,087 257.8
Hauts-de-France 3,782 1,315 875 218.9
Bourgogne-Franche-Comté 3,685 967 930 189.7
Normandie 2,644 858 666 152.4
Île-de-France 1,266 1,056 311 136.7
Centre-Val de Loire 1,754 610 444 105.4
Bretagne 1,202 723 304 102.7
Pays de la Loire 1,228 647 312 95.9
Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur 946 627 229 85.6
Corse 360 130 91 22.2
La Réunion 24 80 6 8.6
Martinique 34 58 8 6.6
Guadeloupe 32 54 8 6.2
Guyane 22 31 5 3.6
Polynésie française 48 8 12 2.0
Nouvelle-Calédonie 33 6 8 1.4
Mayotte 17 3 4 0.7
Terres australes et antarctiques françaises 5 1 1 0.2
Saint-Pierre-et-Miquelon 2 1 0 0.1
Wallis-et-Futuna 3 1 0 0.1
Saint-Martin 1 0 0 0.0
Île des Faisans 1 0 0 0.0
Saint-Barthélemy 1 0 0 0.0
La Passion-Clipperton 1 0 0 0.0

By Department

Breakdown of visit time and travel days for every French department:

Code 
dept_tbl = (dept_df
    .with_columns([
        pl.col("n_towns").alias("Towns"),
        pl.col("total_visit_hours").round(0).cast(pl.Int64).alias("Visit (h)"),
        pl.col("intra_travel_hours").round(0).cast(pl.Int64).alias("Travel (h)"),
        pl.col("total_days").round(1).alias("Days"),
    ])
    .sort("total_days", descending=True)
    .select(["department_name", "region_name", "Towns", "Visit (h)", "Travel (h)", "Days"])
)

dept_tbl.columns = ["Department", "Region", "Towns", "Visit (h)", "Travel (h)", "Days"]

(
    GT(dept_tbl)
    .tab_header("Total days required by department")
    .fmt_number(columns=["Towns", "Visit (h)", "Travel (h)"], decimals=0)
    .fmt_number(columns=["Days"], decimals=1)
    .cols_align("center")
    .opt_row_striping()
    .opt_stylize(style=6)
)
Total days required by department
Department Region Towns Visit (h) Travel (h) Days
Pas-de-Calais Hauts-de-France 887 324 222 54.5
Nord Hauts-de-France 647 381 86 46.7
Moselle Grand Est 725 257 181 43.8
Seine-Maritime Normandie 707 257 176 43.3
Aisne Hauts-de-France 797 197 199 39.6
Isère Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 512 263 128 39.1
Gironde Nouvelle-Aquitaine 534 257 133 39.0
Oise Hauts-de-France 680 220 170 39.0
Somme Hauts-de-France 771 192 192 38.5
Seine-et-Marne Île-de-France 507 256 126 38.2
Haute-Garonne Occitanie 586 222 146 36.8
Bas-Rhin Grand Est 514 217 128 34.5
Côte-d'Or Bourgogne-Franche-Comté 698 168 174 34.3
Meurthe-et-Moselle Grand Est 591 184 148 33.2
Eure Normandie 585 172 146 31.8
Doubs Bourgogne-Franche-Comté 563 176 140 31.7
Pyrénées-Atlantiques Nouvelle-Aquitaine 545 180 136 31.6
Calvados Normandie 526 182 131 31.4
Marne Grand Est 610 155 152 30.7
Saône-et-Loire Bourgogne-Franche-Comté 563 161 140 30.1
Charente-Maritime Nouvelle-Aquitaine 462 182 115 29.7
Puy-de-Dôme Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 463 180 116 29.6
Ain Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 391 174 98 27.1
Dordogne Nouvelle-Aquitaine 503 141 126 26.6
Ille-et-Vilaine Bretagne 332 183 83 26.6
Finistère Bretagne 277 196 69 26.5
Hérault Occitanie 341 180 85 26.5
Vosges Grand Est 506 138 126 26.4
Haut-Rhin Grand Est 366 172 91 26.3
Manche Normandie 445 147 111 25.8
Haute-Saône Bourgogne-Franche-Comté 536 118 134 25.2
Morbihan Bretagne 249 188 62 25.0
Jura Bourgogne-Franche-Comté 492 123 123 24.6
Yvelines Île-de-France 259 182 64 24.6
Gard Occitanie 350 156 87 24.3
Côtes-d'Armor Bretagne 344 156 86 24.2
Haute-Savoie Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 279 172 70 24.2
Aude Occitanie 433 129 108 23.7
Rhône Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 266 200 35 23.6
Hautes-Pyrénées Occitanie 469 115 117 23.2
Loire Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 320 150 80 23.0
Meuse Grand Est 499 106 124 23.0
Loire-Atlantique Pays de la Loire 207 174 52 22.6
Drôme Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 362 130 90 22.0
Loiret Centre-Val de Loire 325 137 81 21.8
Sarthe Pays de la Loire 352 130 88 21.8
Yonne Bourgogne-Franche-Comté 423 113 106 21.8
Aube Grand Est 431 110 108 21.7
Ardennes Grand Est 447 105 112 21.6
Gers Occitanie 458 97 114 21.2
Vendée Pays de la Loire 253 146 63 20.9
Eure-et-Loir Centre-Val de Loire 363 116 90 20.7
Essonne Île-de-France 194 156 48 20.4
Ardèche Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 335 120 84 20.3
Charente Nouvelle-Aquitaine 359 108 90 19.7
Haute-Marne Grand Est 426 91 106 19.7
Orne Normandie 381 100 95 19.5
Savoie Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 273 127 68 19.5
Landes Nouvelle-Aquitaine 327 110 82 19.1
Val-d'Oise Île-de-France 183 146 46 19.1
Indre-et-Loire Centre-Val de Loire 272 121 68 18.8
Tarn Occitanie 314 109 78 18.7
Var Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur 153 144 38 18.2
Allier Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 317 97 79 17.6
Aveyron Occitanie 285 104 71 17.5
Lot-et-Garonne Nouvelle-Aquitaine 319 95 80 17.4
Ariège Occitanie 325 88 81 16.9
Alpes-Maritimes Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur 163 125 40 16.5
Pyrénées-Orientales Occitanie 226 109 56 16.5
Vienne Nouvelle-Aquitaine 265 99 66 16.5
Maine-et-Loire Pays de la Loire 176 118 44 16.2
Bouches-du-Rhône Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur 119 144 16 16.0
Haute-Loire Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 257 96 64 16.0
Corrèze Nouvelle-Aquitaine 277 87 69 15.6
Loir-et-Cher Centre-Val de Loire 267 89 66 15.6
Deux-Sèvres Nouvelle-Aquitaine 252 93 63 15.6
Cher Centre-Val de Loire 286 82 71 15.3
Lot Occitanie 312 76 78 15.3
Nièvre Bourgogne-Franche-Comté 309 75 77 15.2
Cantal Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes 250 78 62 14.0
Mayenne Pays de la Loire 240 79 60 13.9
Haute-Corse Corse 236 77 59 13.6
Vaucluse Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur 151 91 38 12.8
Haute-Vienne Nouvelle-Aquitaine 195 78 48 12.7
Indre Centre-Val de Loire 241 66 60 12.6
Creuse Nouvelle-Aquitaine 255 62 64 12.5
Alpes-de-Haute-Provence Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur 198 67 49 11.6
Tarn-et-Garonne Occitanie 195 65 48 11.4
Seine-Saint-Denis Île-de-France 39 107 5 11.2
Val-de-Marne Île-de-France 47 102 6 10.8
Hauts-de-Seine Île-de-France 36 100 5 10.5
Hautes-Alpes Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur 162 57 40 9.8
Lozère Occitanie 152 48 38 8.6
La Réunion La Réunion 24 80 6 8.6
Corse-du-Sud Corse 124 53 31 8.4
Martinique Martinique 34 58 8 6.6
Guadeloupe Guadeloupe 32 54 8 6.2
Territoire de Belfort Bourgogne-Franche-Comté 101 33 25 5.8
Guyane Guyane 22 31 5 3.6
Polynésie française Polynésie française 48 8 12 2.0
Nouvelle-Calédonie Nouvelle-Calédonie 33 6 8 1.4
Paris Île-de-France 1 8 0 0.8
Mayotte Mayotte 17 3 4 0.7
Terres australes et antarctiques françaises Terres australes et antarctiques françaises 5 1 1 0.2
Saint-Pierre-et-Miquelon Saint-Pierre-et-Miquelon 2 1 0 0.1
Wallis-et-Futuna Wallis-et-Futuna 3 1 0 0.1
Saint-Barthélemy Saint-Barthélemy 1 0 0 0.0
Saint-Martin Saint-Martin 1 0 0 0.0
Île des Faisans Île des Faisans 1 0 0 0.0
La Passion-Clipperton La Passion-Clipperton 1 0 0 0.0

Overseas Territories

Code 
n_overseas_communes = df.filter(pl.col("flag_metropole") == 0).height
display(Markdown(f"The {n_overseas_communes} overseas communes add extra visit + travel days."))

The 224 overseas communes add extra visit + travel days.

Code 
overseas_groups = (
    df.filter((pl.col("flag_metropole") == 0) & (pl.col("overseas_group") != ""))
    .group_by("overseas_group")
    .agg([
        pl.len().alias("n_towns"),
        pl.sum("visit_hours").alias("total_visit_h"),
        pl.max("overseas_travel_days").alias("travel_days"),
    ])
    .with_columns([
        (pl.col("total_visit_h") / 10 + pl.col("travel_days")).round(1).alias("total_days"),
    ])
    .sort("total_days", descending=True)
)

overseas_tbl = overseas_groups.rename({
    "overseas_group": "Territory",
    "n_towns": "Communes",
    "total_visit_h": "Visit (h)",
    "travel_days": "Travel (days)",
    "total_days": "Total days",
})

(
    GT(overseas_tbl)
    .tab_header("Overseas territories — visit time, travel, and total days")
    .fmt_number(columns=["Communes", "Visit (h)", "Travel (days)"], decimals=0)
    .fmt_number(columns=["Total days"], decimals=1)
    .cols_align("center")
    .opt_row_striping()
    .opt_stylize(style=6)
)
Overseas territories — visit time, travel, and total days
Territory Communes Visit (h) Travel (days) Total days
Antilles 68 112 4 15.2
Océan Indien 41 83 4 12.3
Guyane 22 31 4 7.1
Polynésie française 48 8 4 4.8
Nouvelle-Calédonie 33 6 4 4.6
Wallis-et-Futuna 3 1 4 4.1
Saint-Pierre-et-Miquelon 2 1 4 4.1

The Bottom Line

Code 
n_towns = len(df)
n_monuments = int(df["total_monuments"].sum())
n_mountain = int(df["is_mountain"].sum())
n_coast = int(df["is_coast"].sum())
n_insular = int(df["is_insular"].sum())
n_island_commune = int(df["is_island_commune"].sum())
n_overseas_groups = df["overseas_group"].n_unique()
n_full_day = (df["visit_minutes"] >= 480).sum()
years = grand_total_days / 365.25


hero_html = f"""
<div style="
    background: linear-gradient(135deg, #667eea 0%, #764ba2 100%);
    border-radius: 16px;
    padding: 32px 40px;
    text-align: center;
    color: white;
    margin: 24px 0;
    box-shadow: 0 8px 32px rgba(102, 126, 234, 0.4);
">
    <div style="font-size: 72px; font-weight: 800; line-height: 1; margin-bottom: 8px;">
        {grand_total_days:.0f}
    </div>
    <div style="font-size: 20px; font-weight: 300; opacity: 0.9;">
        total days to visit every French commune
    </div>
    <div style="font-size: 14px; margin-top: 8px; opacity: 0.7;">
        {years:.1f} years · 7 days a week · 10 hours a day
    </div>
</div>
"""
display(HTML(hero_html))

md = f"""
**By the numbers:**

- {n_towns:,} communes visited
- {n_monuments:,} historical monuments admired
- {n_mountain:,} mountain towns conquered
- {n_coast:,} coastal towns strolled
- {n_insular:,} communes in island departments/territories
- {n_island_commune:,} mainland island communes
- {n_overseas_groups} overseas territory groups navigated
- {n_full_day:,} communes require a full day (≥ 480 min)

**Fun comparisons:**

- That's roughly **{grand_total_days / 80:.0f}×** the Tour de France (80 days)
- Equivalent to driving **{grand_total_days * 200 / 40075:.0f}×** around the Earth (200 km/day average, 40,075 km circumference)
- **{grand_total_days / 365.25:.1f} years** — could start today and finish in {datetime.now().year + int(years)} at the earliest

**Caveats:**

- Population data is from 2023 — some towns have missing data (visits default to 10 min)
- Travel times are rough estimates (no real routing engine)
- Island commune data is hand-curated from Wikipedia (Îles du Ponant, Belle-Île, Ré, Oléron, Noirmoutier)
- We assumed 7-day weeks and 10-hour days — no rest days, no weekends
- Realistically, you'd need to add 30-50% for meal breaks, wrong turns, and weather
- But hey — it's a fun number!

**What we didn't include:** natural parks and reserves, UNESCO World Heritage sites, sport events (Tour de France stages, marathons, etc.), cultural festivals (Fête de la Musique, film festivals), gastronomic routes, and local markets — all of which would add significant time to any real visit.
"""
display(Markdown(md))
2248
total days to visit every French commune
6.2 years · 7 days a week · 10 hours a day

By the numbers:

  • 34,970 communes visited
  • 45,610 historical monuments admired
  • 5,579 mountain towns conquered
  • 947 coastal towns strolled
  • 561 communes in island departments/territories
  • 38 mainland island communes
  • 9 overseas territory groups navigated
  • 42 communes require a full day (≥ 480 min)

Fun comparisons:

  • That’s roughly 28× the Tour de France (80 days)
  • Equivalent to driving 11× around the Earth (200 km/day average, 40,075 km circumference)
  • 6.2 years — could start today and finish in 2032 at the earliest

Caveats:

  • Population data is from 2023 — some towns have missing data (visits default to 10 min)
  • Travel times are rough estimates (no real routing engine)
  • Island commune data is hand-curated from Wikipedia (Îles du Ponant, Belle-Île, Ré, Oléron, Noirmoutier)
  • We assumed 7-day weeks and 10-hour days — no rest days, no weekends
  • Realistically, you’d need to add 30-50% for meal breaks, wrong turns, and weather
  • But hey — it’s a fun number!

What we didn’t include: natural parks and reserves, UNESCO World Heritage sites, sport events (Tour de France stages, marathons, etc.), cultural festivals (Fête de la Musique, film festivals), gastronomic routes, and local markets — all of which would add significant time to any real visit.

Conclusion

Code 
conclusion = f"""
Visiting every French commune is a monumental undertaking: **{grand_total_days:.0f} days ({years:.1f} years)** of non-stop travel, with 10-hour days and no rest. The biggest drivers are the sheer number of towns ({n_towns:,}), mountain terrain adding 30% more time, and overseas logistics requiring dedicated trips. While the estimate is rough, it gives a concrete sense of scale. This does't account for anything else than visiting towns, which isnt reaalistic. Sounds like a project for a lifetime!
"""
display(Markdown(conclusion))

Visiting every French commune is a monumental undertaking: 2248 days (6.2 years) of non-stop travel, with 10-hour days and no rest. The biggest drivers are the sheer number of towns (34,970), mountain terrain adding 30% more time, and overseas logistics requiring dedicated trips. While the estimate is rough, it gives a concrete sense of scale. This does’t account for anything else than visiting towns, which isnt reaalistic. Sounds like a project for a lifetime!

item.title: What’s in a French Commune Name?
item.description:
This post runs live queries against the lakehouse’s gold layer, which are Iceberg tables served via Apache Polaris and DuckDB.

The article queries a single table and carries factoid analysis of French commune names. The goal of this post is making sure we can connect to the lakehouse using quarto and our connection function sql_to_df.

0. Lakehouse connection

We import sql_to_df and the libraries for data display:

Code 
from great_tables import GT, loc, style
from itables import show
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import polars as pl

from blog_utils import sql_to_df

1. Name length distribution

Let’s start looking at how long French commune names tend to be

Code 
query_length_distribution = """
    SELECT LENGTH(name) AS "Name lenght", COUNT(*) AS "Number of Communes"
    FROM gold.dim_communes
    GROUP BY "Name lenght"
    ORDER BY "Name lenght"
"""
df_length_distribution = sql_to_df(query_length_distribution, dataframe_type="pandas")
show(df_length_distribution, caption="Name Length in french Communes")
Loading ITables v2.7.3 from the internet... (need help?)

The shortest name is 1 character (Y), the longest is 45 characters (Saint-Remy-en-Bouzemont-Saint-Genest-et-Isson).

But characters include spaces and dashes. what if we only look for letters?

Code 
query_letter_distribution = """
    SELECT LENGTH(REGEXP_REPLACE(name, '\P{L}', '', 'g')) AS "Letter count",
    COUNT(*) AS "Number of Communes"
    FROM gold.dim_communes
    GROUP BY "Letter count"
    ORDER BY "Letter count"
"""
df_letter_distribution = sql_to_df(query_letter_distribution, dataframe_type="pandas")
show(df_letter_distribution, caption="Number of letters in French Communes")
Loading ITables v2.7.3 from the internet... (need help?)

Two towns have 38 letters, they are Saint-Remy-en-Bouzemont-Saint-Genest-et-Isson and Beaujeu-Saint-Vallier-Pierrejux-et-Quitteur. How long these names are!

We can also perform some summary statistics on the communes lengths:

Code 
query_length_stats = """
    SELECT
        MIN(LENGTH(name))          AS min_len,
        CAST(AVG(LENGTH(name)) AS DECIMAL(5,1)) AS avg_len,
        CAST(MEDIAN(LENGTH(name)) AS INTEGER)    AS med_len,
        MAX(LENGTH(name))          AS max_len
    FROM gold.dim_communes
"""
df_length_stats = sql_to_df(query_length_stats, dataframe_type="pandas")
df_length_stats
min_len avg_len med_len max_len
0 1 11.6 10 45

On average, we have a length (including spaces and dashes) of 11.8 characters, and a median value of 10 characters. Let’s take a look at shortest and longest commune names.

Code 
query_shortest_names = """
    SELECT name, LENGTH(name) AS len
    FROM gold.dim_communes
    ORDER BY len
    LIMIT 5
"""
df_shortest_names = sql_to_df(query_shortest_names, dataframe_type="pandas")

(
    GT(df_shortest_names)
    .tab_header(
        title="Shortest Commune Names in France",
        subtitle="Top 5 communes by name length (raw characters)"
    )
    .cols_label(
        name="Commune Name",
        len="Length (chars)"
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="len",
        decimals=0,
        use_seps=False
    )
    .data_color(
        columns="len",
        palette=["#e8f5e9", "#4caf50", "#2e7d32"]
    )
    .tab_source_note("Source: gold.dim_communes")
)
Shortest Commune Names in France
Top 5 communes by name length (raw characters)
Commune Name Length (chars)
Y 1
Oz 2
Sy 2
Eu 2
Ry 2
Source: gold.dim_communes
Code 
query_longest_names = """
    SELECT name, LENGTH(name) AS len
    FROM gold.dim_communes
    ORDER BY len DESC
    LIMIT 5
"""
df_longest_names = sql_to_df(query_longest_names, dataframe_type="pandas")

(
    GT(df_longest_names)
    .tab_header(
        title="Longest Commune Names in France",
        subtitle="Top 5 communes by name length (raw characters)"
    )
    .cols_label(
        name="Commune Name",
        len="Length (chars)"
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="len",
        decimals=0,
        use_seps=False
    )
    .data_color(
        columns="len",
        palette=["#FFD700", "#FF8C00", "#FF0000"]
    )
    .tab_source_note("Source: gold.dim_communes")
)
Longest Commune Names in France
Top 5 communes by name length (raw characters)
Commune Name Length (chars)
Saint-Remy-en-Bouzemont-Saint-Genest-et-Isson 45
Beaujeu-Saint-Vallier-Pierrejux-et-Quitteur 43
Saint-Martin-de-Bienfaite-la-Cressonnière 41
Montigny-Mornay-Villeneuve-sur-Vingeanne 40
Roche-sur-Linotte-et-Sorans-les-Cordiers 40
Source: gold.dim_communes

Looking at the full distribution, the 6-10 character length range is by far the most common bin in the histogram:

Code 
# Histogram: group into buckets for readability
buckets = [0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 999]
labels = ["1-5", "6-10", "11-15", "16-20", "21-25", "26-30", "31-35", "36-40", "41+"]
df_length_distribution["bucket"] = pd.cut(
    df_length_distribution["Name lenght"], bins=buckets, labels=labels, right=True
)
hist = df_length_distribution.groupby("bucket", observed=True)["Number of Communes"].sum()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5))
bars = ax.bar(hist.index, hist.values, color="#2c7fb8", edgecolor="white")
ax.set_xlabel("Name length (characters)")
ax.set_ylabel("Number of communes")
ax.set_title("Distribution of French Commune Name Lengths", fontweight="bold")
ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
for bar, val in zip(bars, hist.values):
    ax.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, bar.get_height() + 200,
            str(val), ha="center", fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

2. Most common commune names

Let’s explore which commune names appear most frequently across France. Many towns share the same name, often reflecting religious heritage (Saint names), geographic features, or historical figures.

2.1 Repeated commune names

Code 
query_most_common_names = """
    SELECT name, COUNT(*) AS occurrences
    FROM gold.dim_communes
    GROUP BY name
    HAVING occurrences > 8
    ORDER BY occurrences DESC
"""
df_most_common_names = sql_to_df(query_most_common_names, dataframe_type="pandas")

(
    GT(df_most_common_names)
    .tab_header(
        title="Most Frequent Commune Names in France",
        subtitle="Names appearing more than 8 times across the country"
    )
    .cols_label(
        name="Commune Name",
        occurrences="Number of Communes"
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="occurrences",
        decimals=0,
        use_seps=True
    )
    .data_color(
        columns="occurrences",
        palette=["#FFD700", "#FF8C00", "#FF0000"]
    )
    .tab_style(
        style=style.text(weight="bold"),
        locations=loc.body(rows=[0], columns="occurrences")
    )
    .tab_source_note("Source: gold.dim_communes")
)
Most Frequent Commune Names in France
Names appearing more than 8 times across the country
Commune Name Number of Communes
Sainte-Colombe 12
Saint-Sauveur 11
Pin 10
Beaulieu 10
Saint-Aubin 10
Saint-Pierre 9
Saint-Marcel 9
Sainte-Marie 9
Saint-Michel 9
Source: gold.dim_communes

2.2 Most common words

Beyond exact name duplicates, many communes share common prefixes or suffixes. Let’s split each commune name into individual words (ignoring hyphens) and see which components appear most frequently. We’ll ignore French stopwords.

Code 
# French stopwords (common words to exclude)
french_stopwords = {
    "le", "la", "les", "un", "une", "des", "du", "de", "d'", "l'",
    "et", "ou", "mais", "donc", "or", "ni", "car",
    "sur", "sous", "dans", "avec", "sans", "par", "pour", "en", "vers", "entre",
    "au", "aux", "chez", "après", "avant", "pendant", "depuis", "jusque",
    "ce", "ces", "cet", "cette", "mon", "ton", "son", "notre", "votre", "leur",
    "a", "à", "est", "sont", "était", "étaient", "être", "avoir", "ont",
    "faire", "fait", "parce", "que", "qui", "qu'", "dont", "où", "quoi",
    "je", "tu", "il", "elle", "nous", "vous", "ils", "elles",
    "me", "te", "se", "y", "en", "lui", "leur"
}

query_all_names = """
    SELECT name
    FROM gold.dim_communes
"""
df_names = sql_to_df(query_all_names, dataframe_type="polars")

# Split names into words, handling spaces and hyphens
word_counts = (
    df_names
    .select(
        pl.col("name")
        .str.replace_all("-", " ")  # Replace hyphens with spaces
        .str.replace_all("'", " ")  # Handle apostrophes (e.g., l'Église)
        .str.split(" ")
        .alias("words")
    )
    .explode("words")
    .filter(
        pl.col("words").str.len_chars() > 1,  # Filter out single characters
        ~pl.col("words").str.to_lowercase().is_in(french_stopwords),  # Exclude stopwords
        pl.col("words").is_not_null(),
        pl.col("words") != ""  # Remove empty strings
    )
    .with_columns(
        # Capitalize first letter for cleaner display
        pl.col("words").str.to_titlecase().alias("words")
    )
    .group_by("words")
    .len()
    .sort("len", descending=True)
    .limit(15)
)

(
    GT(word_counts)
    .tab_header(
        title="Most Common Name Components",
        subtitle="Top 15 meaningful words in French commune names (stopwords excluded)"
    )
    .cols_label(
        words="Name Component",
        len="Frequency"
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="len",
        decimals=0,
        use_seps=True
    )
    .data_color(
        columns="len",
        palette=["#FFD700", "#FF8C00", "#FF0000"]
    )
    .tab_source_note("Analysis based on splitting names by spaces, hyphens, and apostrophes. Excludes common French stopwords and words shorter than 2 characters.")
)
Most Common Name Components
Top 15 meaningful words in French commune names (stopwords excluded)
Name Component Frequency
Saint 4,090
Lès 548
Sainte 359
Martin 256
Bois 218
Pierre 180
Chapelle 178
Jean 171
Val 132
Germain 120
Mer 109
Villers 108
Mont 103
Château 95
Ville 93
Analysis based on splitting names by spaces, hyphens, and apostrophes. Excludes common French stopwords and words shorter than 2 characters.

2.3 Saint names analysis

Given how many French communes begin with “Saint-”, let’s analyze the most popular patron saints:

Code 
query_saint_names = """
    SELECT
        name,
        REGEXP_EXTRACT(name, '^Saint[- ](.+?)(?:$|[- ])', 1) AS saint_name
    FROM gold.dim_communes
    WHERE name LIKE 'Saint-%' OR name LIKE 'Saint %'
"""
df_saints = sql_to_df(query_saint_names, dataframe_type="polars")

saint_counts = (
    df_saints
    .filter(
        pl.col("saint_name").is_not_null(),
        ~pl.col("saint_name").str.to_lowercase().is_in(french_stopwords)
    )
    .with_columns(
        # Handle compound saint names (e.g., "Jean Baptiste" -> keep as is)
        pl.col("saint_name").str.strip_chars()
    )
    .group_by("saint_name")
    .len()
    .sort("len", descending=True)
    .limit(10)
)

(
    GT(saint_counts)
    .tab_header(
        title="Most Popular Patron Saints",
        subtitle="Top 10 saints honored in French commune names"
    )
    .cols_label(
        saint_name="Saint Name",
        len="Number of Communes"
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="len",
        decimals=0,
        use_seps=True
    )
    .data_color(
        columns="len",
        palette=["#FFD700", "#FF8C00", "#FF0000"]
    )
    .tab_source_note("Based on communes starting with 'Saint-' or 'Saint '")
)
Most Popular Patron Saints
Top 10 saints honored in French commune names
Saint Name Number of Communes
Martin 204
Jean 156
Pierre 144
Germain 108
Julien 78
Laurent 77
Hilaire 67
Georges 67
Étienne 65
André 64
Based on communes starting with 'Saint-' or 'Saint '

2.4 Geographic indicators

Finally, let’s look at common geographic qualifiers that appear in commune names:

Code 
query_geo_terms = """
    SELECT name
    FROM gold.dim_communes
"""
df_geo = sql_to_df(query_geo_terms, dataframe_type="polars")

# More comprehensive geographic patterns
geo_patterns = {
    "sur": "situated on (river/hill)",
    "sous": "under/below",
    "en": "in",
    "les": "the (plural)",
    "le": "the (masculine)",
    "la": "the (feminine)",
    "près": "near",
    "devant": "in front of",
    "derrière": "behind",
    "entre": "between"
}

geo_counts = []
for pattern, description in geo_patterns.items():
    count = df_geo.filter(pl.col("name").str.contains(f"-{pattern}-| {pattern} ")).height
    geo_counts.append({"qualifier": pattern, "meaning": description, "count": count})

df_geo_terms = pl.DataFrame(geo_counts).sort("count", descending=True)

(
    GT(df_geo_terms)
    .tab_header(
        title="Common Geographic Qualifiers",
        subtitle="Prepositional indicators found in French commune names"
    )
    .cols_label(
        qualifier="Qualifier",
        meaning="Meaning",
        count="Number of Communes"
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="count",
        decimals=0,
        use_seps=True
    )
    .data_color(
        columns="count",
        palette=["#FFD700", "#FF8C00", "#FF0000"]
    )
    .tab_source_note("Examples: 'Villefranche-sur-Saône', 'Saint-Germain-en-Laye', 'Bourg-la-Reine'")
)
Common Geographic Qualifiers
Prepositional indicators found in French commune names
Qualifier Meaning Number of Communes
sur situated on (river/hill) 2,006
en in 1,006
le the (masculine) 823
la the (feminine) 662
les the (plural) 469
sous under/below 257
près near 46
devant in front of 25
derrière behind 2
entre between 2
Examples: 'Villefranche-sur-Saône', 'Saint-Germain-en-Laye', 'Bourg-la-Reine'

3. Most common first letters

The first letter of a commune name can reveal interesting patterns about French toponymy. Let’s analyze which letters are most popular at the beginning of commune names.

Code 
query_first_letters = """
    SELECT
        UPPER(LEFT(name, 1)) AS letter,
        COUNT(*) AS communes,
        ROUND(COUNT(*) * 100.0 / SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER(), 1) AS percentage
    FROM gold.dim_communes
    GROUP BY letter
    ORDER BY communes DESC
"""
df_first_letters = sql_to_df(query_first_letters, dataframe_type="pandas")

# Display with Great Tables
(
    GT(df_first_letters.head(15))
    .tab_header(
        title="Most Common First Letters",
        subtitle="Top 15 initial letters across all French commune names"
    )
    .cols_label(
        letter="First Letter",
        communes="Number of Communes",
        percentage="Percentage (%)"
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="communes",
        decimals=0,
        use_seps=True
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="percentage",
        decimals=1,
        pattern="{x}%"  # Add % suffix using pattern instead of suffix parameter
    )
    .data_color(
        columns="communes",
        palette=["#e8f5e9", "#4caf50", "#2e7d32"]
    )
    .tab_style(
        style=style.text(weight="bold"),
        locations=loc.body(rows=[0, 1, 2], columns="letter")
    )
    .tab_source_note("Source: gold.dim_communes")
)
Most Common First Letters
Top 15 initial letters across all French commune names
First Letter Number of Communes Percentage (%)
S 5,676 16.2%
C 4,123 11.8%
B 3,441 9.8%
M 3,351 9.6%
V 2,248 6.4%
L 2,125 6.1%
P 1,994 5.7%
A 1,910 5.5%
R 1,326 3.8%
G 1,307 3.7%
F 1,282 3.7%
T 1,242 3.6%
N 772 2.2%
D 770 2.2%
H 761 2.2%
Source: gold.dim_communes

Visualizing the distribution

Code 
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.bar(df_first_letters["letter"], df_first_letters["communes"], color="#2ca02c", edgecolor="white")
ax.set_xlabel("First letter")
ax.set_ylabel("Number of communes")
ax.set_title("Most Common First Letters", fontweight="bold")
ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Letters that never appear

What about letters that never (or rarely) start a commune name?

Code 
query_missing_letters = """
    WITH all_letters AS (
        SELECT UNNEST(REGEXP_SPLIT_TO_ARRAY('ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ', '')) AS letter
    ),
    used_letters AS (
        SELECT DISTINCT UPPER(LEFT(name, 1)) AS letter
        FROM gold.dim_communes
    )
    SELECT letter
    FROM all_letters
    WHERE letter NOT IN (SELECT letter FROM used_letters WHERE letter IS NOT NULL)
    ORDER BY letter
"""
df_missing_letters = sql_to_df(query_missing_letters, dataframe_type="pandas")

if len(df_missing_letters) > 0:
    print(f"📝 Letters that NEVER start a French commune name: {', '.join(df_missing_letters['letter'])}")
else:
    print("✅ All letters of the alphabet appear at least once as a first letter!")
✅ All letters of the alphabet appear at least once as a first letter!

4. Department-level analysis

Different departments have distinct naming patterns. Let’s explore which departments have the longest commune names on average, and visualize the distribution of name lengths within the top departments.

4.1 Departments with longest average commune names

Code 
query_dept_avg_length = """
    SELECT
        department_name,
        ROUND(AVG(LENGTH(name)), 1) AS avg_length,
        COUNT(*) AS communes,
        MIN(LENGTH(name)) AS min_length,
        MAX(LENGTH(name)) AS max_length
    FROM gold.dim_communes
    GROUP BY department_name
    ORDER BY avg_length DESC
    LIMIT 15
"""
df_dept_avg_length = sql_to_df(query_dept_avg_length, dataframe_type="pandas")

(
    GT(df_dept_avg_length)
    .tab_header(
        title="Departments with Longest Commune Names",
        subtitle="Top 15 departments by average name length (raw characters)"
    )
    .cols_label(
        department_name="Department",
        avg_length="Avg. Length",
        communes="Number of Communes",
        min_length="Min Length",
        max_length="Max Length"
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="avg_length",
        decimals=1
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns=["communes", "min_length", "max_length"],
        decimals=0,
        use_seps=True
    )
    .data_color(
        columns="avg_length",
        palette=["#FFD700", "#FF8C00", "#FF0000"]
    )
    .tab_source_note("Source: gold.dim_communes")
)
Departments with Longest Commune Names
Top 15 departments by average name length (raw characters)
Department Avg. Length Number of Communes Min Length Max Length
Terres australes et antarctiques françaises 22.2 5 12 30
La Passion-Clipperton 21.0 1 21 21
Saint-Barthélemy 16.0 1 16 16
Île des Faisans 15.0 1 15 15
Saint-Pierre-et-Miquelon 14.5 2 12 17
Dordogne 14.0 503 4 38
Vendée 13.9 253 3 35
Seine-Maritime 13.8 707 2 31
Lozère 13.6 152 3 34
Maine-et-Loire 13.6 176 4 29
Eure 13.5 585 4 29
Meuse 13.4 499 3 28
Orne 13.4 381 2 32
Haute-Vienne 13.3 195 4 26
Val-de-Marne 13.2 47 4 24
Source: gold.dim_communes

4.2 Distribution of name lengths for top departments

Code 
# Box plot: name lengths for top 12 departments
top12 = df_dept_avg_length["department_name"].head(12).tolist()

dept_data = []
for d in top12:
    rows_df = sql_to_df(
        "SELECT LENGTH(name) AS len FROM gold.dim_communes "
        "WHERE department_name = ? ORDER BY len",
        [d],
        dataframe_type="pandas",
    )
    dept_data.append(rows_df["len"].tolist())

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))
bp = ax.boxplot(dept_data, tick_labels=[d[:20] for d in top12], patch_artist=True)
for patch in bp["boxes"]:
    patch.set_facecolor("#2c7fb8")
    patch.set_alpha(0.7)
for median in bp["medians"]:
    median.set_color("black")
    median.set_linewidth(2)
ax.set_xlabel("Department")
ax.set_ylabel("Commune name length (characters)")
ax.set_title("Commune Name Lengths by Department", fontweight="bold")
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=45)
ax.spines["top"].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["right"].set_visible(False)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

4.3 Which department has the shortest average names?

Code 
query_dept_shortest = """
    SELECT
        department_name,
        ROUND(AVG(LENGTH(name)), 1) AS avg_length,
        COUNT(*) AS communes
    FROM gold.dim_communes
    GROUP BY department_name
    ORDER BY avg_length ASC
    LIMIT 10
"""
df_dept_shortest = sql_to_df(query_dept_shortest, dataframe_type="pandas")

(
    GT(df_dept_shortest)
    .tab_header(
        title="Departments with Shortest Commune Names",
        subtitle="Top 10 departments by shortest average name length"
    )
    .cols_label(
        department_name="Department",
        avg_length="Avg. Length",
        communes="Number of Communes"
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="avg_length",
        decimals=1
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="communes",
        decimals=0,
        use_seps=True
    )
    .data_color(
        columns="avg_length",
        palette=["#e8f5e9", "#4caf50", "#2e7d32"]
    )
    .tab_source_note("Source: gold.dim_communes")
)
Departments with Shortest Commune Names
Top 10 departments by shortest average name length
Department Avg. Length Number of Communes
Wallis-et-Futuna 4.3 3
Paris 5.0 1
Nouvelle-Calédonie 6.6 33
Polynésie française 7.3 48
Mayotte 7.9 17
Hautes-Pyrénées 8.7 469
Corse-du-Sud 9.2 124
Morbihan 9.3 249
Guyane 9.6 22
Ariège 9.6 325
Source: gold.dim_communes

5. Most communes per department

Not all departments are created equal when it comes to the number of communes. Let’s see which departments have the highest number of communes.

Code 
query_most_communes_dept = """
    SELECT
        department_name,
        COUNT(*) AS communes,
        ROUND(AVG(LENGTH(name)), 1) AS avg_name_length,
        ROUND(STDDEV(LENGTH(name)), 1) AS stddev_length
    FROM gold.dim_communes
    GROUP BY department_name
    ORDER BY communes DESC
    LIMIT 10
"""
df_most_communes_dept = sql_to_df(query_most_communes_dept, dataframe_type="pandas")

(
    GT(df_most_communes_dept)
    .tab_header(
        title="Departments with Most Communes",
        subtitle="Top 10 departments by total number of communes"
    )
    .cols_label(
        department_name="Department",
        communes="Number of Communes",
        avg_name_length="Avg. Name Length",
        stddev_length="Std. Deviation"
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns=["communes", "stddev_length"],
        decimals=0,
        use_seps=True
    )
    .fmt_number(
        columns="avg_name_length",
        decimals=1
    )
    .data_color(
        columns="communes",
        palette=["#f3e5f5", "#9c27b0", "#4a148c"]
    )
    .tab_style(
        style=style.text(weight="bold"),
        locations=loc.body(rows=[0], columns="communes")
    )
    .tab_source_note("Source: gold.dim_communes")
)
Departments with Most Communes
Top 10 departments by total number of communes
Department Number of Communes Avg. Name Length Std. Deviation
Pas-de-Calais 887 11.0 5
Aisne 797 11.5 5
Somme 771 11.3 5
Moselle 725 10.3 4
Seine-Maritime 707 13.8 6
Côte-d'Or 698 12.0 6
Oise 680 11.7 5
Nord 647 10.6 5
Marne 610 11.8 6
Meurthe-et-Moselle 591 11.2 5
Source: gold.dim_communes

Conclusion

This article is completely useless.

But it’s also great fun! Sometimes the best part of working with data is following your curiosity down rabbit holes, asking silly questions, and seeing what interesting (if impractical) patterns emerge.

French commune names tell a story of saints, rivers, history and geography, of hyphenated mouthfuls and mysterious single-letter towns like “Y”. We’ve learned that:

  • S dominates as a first letter (blame Saint- everywhere!)
  • 6-10 characters is the sweet spot for name length
  • Saint-Martin is everywhere (sorry, other saints)
  • And Vendée ❤️ apparently likes long, winding names

I hope you had as much fun reading this as I did running the queries. 🥖🇫🇷

Data source: French commune registry via Iceberg tables, served through Apache Polaris and DuckDB. Queries run live when generating the article (and it’s uploaded in fixed state).

item.title: Hello World, Introducing the French Towns Lakehouse
item.description:
Welcome to the French Towns Lakehouse blog.

This project builds a self-hosted data lakehouse for all 35,000+ French communes. It downloads open government datasets (INSEE, Laposte, data.gouv.fr) and scrapes municipal label sites (flower cities, sports towns, heritage villages), then transforms everything into clean, queryable Parquet files using DuckDB and dbt.

The blog has two main sections:

  • Engineering , deep dives into the tech stack, data modelling decisions, pipeline architecture, and infrastructure.
  • Insights , analysis and findings from the data itself.

The dbt documentation shows the full data lineage and model definitions.

All source code is on GitHub: github.com/enarroied/french_towns_lakehouse.

Stay tuned for more posts.
